hero.title: plugin-kit-ai
hero.subtitle: Build a plugin once. Ship it to multiple AI agents from one repo. plugin-kit-ai is the home for our first-party plugin lineup and the open-source toolkit behind it.

features.title: One repo instead of scattered setup
features.description: Keep one plugin repo as the source of truth instead of copying the same logic into separate agent-specific folders.

features.title: Start with one strong path, add more later
features.description: Start by building the plugin for the one agent you need right now, then expand to more supported agents when your product actually needs it.

features.title: Validate before handoff
features.description: Use a repeatable generate and validate flow before another teammate, CI job, or end user touches the plugin.

features.title: Honest stable vs beta boundary
features.description: plugin-kit-ai is explicit about what is production-ready, what is public beta, and where you should not promise fake parity yet.

features.title: Clear install and release path
features.description: Homebrew, npm, pipx when published, verified install script, GitHub releases, and docs all point to one clean starting flow.

features.title: Open source from day one
features.description: Use the toolkit today, inspect the source, and follow a transparent workflow while our first-party plugin catalog grows.

plugins.title: Context7
plugins.tagline: Fresh library docs and code examples without tab-switching back to vendor docs.
plugins.description: A real multi-agent MCP plugin for fresh library docs and code examples, generated once and shipped to Claude, Codex, Gemini, OpenCode, and Cursor.
highlights: Pulls current library docs instead of relying on stale model memory.
Puts examples and docs in one MCP surface that works across supported agents.
Fits best when teams need fast technical lookup inside active repo work.
useCases: Verifying the latest SDK syntax before writing code.
Finding examples for a framework feature without leaving the editor.
Cross-checking generated code against current docs during reviews.

plugins.title: GitLab
plugins.tagline: Project, merge-request, and DevOps workflows from one GitLab-native MCP surface.
plugins.description: A real GitLab MCP plugin for projects, issues, merge requests, and DevOps workflows through the official GitLab MCP endpoint.
highlights: Keeps issues, merge requests, and project state inside the same agent workflow.
Useful for teams already centered on GitLab as the operational source of truth.
Bridges repo work with planning and deployment-related actions.
useCases: Triaging issues and moving linked work forward from the same context.
Reviewing merge requests while checking related project state.
Coordinating DevOps follow-up without leaving the MCP flow.

plugins.title: GitHub
plugins.tagline: Repository, issue, and pull-request operations through the official GitHub MCP service.
plugins.description: A real GitHub MCP plugin for repository, issues, and pull-request workflows through the official GitHub MCP service with a configured authorization token.
highlights: Covers the core repo workflow most engineering teams touch every day.
Works well when issue tracking and pull-request review both live in GitHub.
Turns GitHub into a first-class MCP action surface rather than a context switch.
useCases: Reviewing pull requests with related issue context nearby.
Managing repository housekeeping, issues, and PR follow-up in one flow.
Handing off planning and review work between teammates and agents.

plugins.title: Firebase
plugins.tagline: Firestore, Auth, Functions, Hosting, and Storage operations from one backend-focused plugin.
plugins.description: A real Firebase MCP plugin for project operations across Firestore, Auth, Functions, Hosting, and Storage via `firebase-tools mcp`.
highlights: Best when product teams already run app backend pieces through Firebase.
Connects data, auth, and deployment-oriented operations in one plugin surface.
Useful for quickly moving between schema, functions, and hosting tasks.
useCases: Checking Firestore and Auth setup while iterating on app features.
Coordinating Functions and Hosting changes from one MCP workflow.
Supporting small teams that want one backend-oriented plugin instead of many tools.

plugins.title: Linear
plugins.tagline: Issue planning and workflow automation for teams that already run on Linear.
plugins.description: A real Linear MCP plugin for teams using issues, project planning, and workflow automation via the official Linear MCP endpoint.
highlights: Keeps planning and execution close together for teams already living in Linear.
Strong fit for issue hygiene, project progress, and workflow automation loops.
Useful when agents should help move work, not just summarize it.
useCases: Updating issue state during implementation and review.
Tracking project momentum without bouncing between planning tools.
Automating routine workflow steps around assignment and status.

plugins.title: Notion
plugins.tagline: Workspace docs, planning context, and team knowledge through Notion's hosted MCP service.
plugins.description: A real Notion MCP plugin for user-authorized workspace access through Notion's hosted MCP endpoint with one remote OAuth-based integration across Claude, Codex, Gemini, OpenCode, and Cursor.
highlights: Best when product, specs, and team knowledge already live in Notion.
Keeps interactive workspace context inside the same agent flow through hosted MCP and OAuth.
Useful for reading, searching, and updating planning context without building a separate local integration.
useCases: Pulling project context from team docs before making code or product changes.
Checking specs, launch notes, or operating docs without leaving the agent workspace.
Updating shared planning pages during execution and follow-up.

plugins.title: Stripe
plugins.tagline: Hosted OAuth-first MCP access to payments, billing, customers, and Stripe knowledge, with advanced token and local paths intentionally kept out of v1.
plugins.description: A real Stripe MCP plugin for hosted access to payments, billing, customer, and documentation workflows through Stripe's official remote MCP endpoint.
highlights: Strong fit for teams that need agents to reason about real billing and commerce state, not only docs.
Keeps the community default clean with hosted OAuth instead of baking restricted API keys into generated config.
Explicitly treats local and autonomous token-driven modes as separate advanced paths rather than quietly mixing them into one plugin.
useCases: Checking customers, products, subscriptions, invoices, and refunds from the same workspace as code and ops work.
Pulling Stripe docs and support knowledge into the same flow as account operations.
Helping human-approved agents handle recurring billing and payments context without extra local setup.

plugins.title: Vercel
plugins.tagline: Hosted MCP access to Vercel docs, projects, deployments, and logs while the upstream service is still in beta.
plugins.description: A real Vercel MCP plugin for OAuth-backed hosted access to documentation, projects, deployments, and logs through Vercel's official remote MCP endpoint.
highlights: Strong fit for teams already shipping and operating through Vercel.
Combines docs lookup with authenticated deployment and project workflows in one MCP surface.
Keeps the plugin honest about upstream beta status instead of pretending the service is fully settled.
useCases: Checking deployment state and logs while debugging production behavior.
Pulling Vercel docs into the same workflow as project operations.
Helping agents reason about platform and deployment context without leaving the workspace.

plugins.title: Sentry
plugins.tagline: Human-in-the-loop debugging and incident context through Sentry's hosted MCP service.
plugins.description: A real Sentry MCP plugin for hosted OAuth-backed access to issues, traces, debugging context, and incident workflows through Sentry's official remote MCP service.
highlights: Built for engineering teams that need real production debugging context, not just docs.
Keeps issue, trace, and incident context close to the coding workflow.
Deliberately scoped to the hosted SaaS path instead of mixing in self-hosted and stdio complexity.
useCases: Triage production issues while reviewing code or planning fixes.
Use traces and issue context to narrow likely failure paths faster.
Support incident follow-up without bouncing between dashboards and agent sessions.

plugins.title: Supabase
plugins.tagline: Project-scoped backend development through a Supabase-hosted MCP surface.
plugins.description: A real Supabase MCP plugin for project-scoped development workflows through Supabase-hosted MCP.
highlights: Designed for teams building app backends with Supabase as the center of gravity.
Makes project-scoped database and platform work easier to keep in one flow.
Pairs well with product teams that need backend speed without a huge ops layer.
useCases: Checking project setup while iterating on database-backed features.
Moving between backend tasks without leaving the agent workspace.
Supporting teams that prefer one managed backend surface for rapid product work.

plugins.title: Greptile
plugins.tagline: Repository-aware code intelligence for deep search, context gathering, and review support.
plugins.description: A real Greptile MCP plugin for repository-aware code intelligence via Greptile hosted MCP.
highlights: Helps agents inspect repository context instead of guessing from partial files.
Strong fit for deep code search, understanding unfamiliar areas, and review prep.
Works well when fast repository comprehension matters as much as raw generation.
useCases: Exploring an unfamiliar codebase before making changes.
Finding related implementation patterns across a repository during review.
Building better technical context for debugging and refactoring.

comparisonRows.feature: One repo for many agent outputs
Add outputs later without starting over

duplicated.note: Every new output starts from scratch

pluginKitAi.note: Docs, releases, and install paths line up

faq.question: What is plugin-kit-ai?
faq.answer: plugin-kit-ai is an open-source toolkit for keeping one plugin repo as the source of truth, then generating and validating the supported agent outputs you actually need.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: Use it when you want a real plugin repo, a shared workflow, and a cleaner path than one-off scripts or duplicated agent-specific config.

faq.question: Do I need one repo per agent?
faq.answer: No. The point is to keep one authored source repo, then target supported outputs for Claude, Codex, Gemini, OpenCode, Cursor, and others from that same base instead of rebuilding each one from scratch.

faq.question: Is every supported output equally stable today?
faq.answer: No. Some lanes are production-ready, and some remain public beta. plugin-kit-ai is explicit about that boundary so you can make honest product promises.

faq.question: What is the fastest way to start?
faq.answer: Install plugin-kit-ai, create a repo with init, then run render and validate --strict. Most teams should start with the Quickstart. If your team is intentionally Python-first, go straight to the Python guide.

faq.question: Which path should I start with: Go, Python, or Node?
faq.answer: Start with Go when you want the strongest default path. Choose Node/TypeScript for the main supported non-Go path. Choose Python when the repo stays local and your team is already Python-first.

download.title: Get started
download.note: Pick an install path, open the docs, and start from one clean plugin repo.

installChannels.title: Homebrew
installChannels.description: Recommended local install for machines that already use Homebrew.
installChannels.note: Best default when Homebrew is available.

installChannels.title: npm
installChannels.description: Official JavaScript ecosystem wrapper for teams that already center their workflow on npm.
installChannels.note: Public-beta wrapper that downloads the matching verified release binary.

installChannels.title: pipx
installChannels.description: Python ecosystem wrapper when the corresponding release is published to PyPI.
installChannels.note: Public-beta wrapper. Use this channel only when the target release is available on PyPI.

installChannels.title: Verified install script
installChannels.description: Fallback bootstrap path that resolves the latest published stable release and verifies checksums.
installChannels.note: Good when package-manager setup is not what you want first.

installChannels.title: GitHub Releases
installChannels.description: Browse published release notes, artifacts, and version history directly.
installChannels.note: Use this when you want the raw release surface.

installChannels.title: Docs quickstart
installChannels.description: Jump straight into the quickstart and the broader docs site.
installChannels.note: Best next step once the CLI is installed.

quickstartSteps.title: Install the CLI
quickstartSteps.note: Homebrew is the recommended local install path for the CLI itself.

quickstartSteps.title: Create the strongest default repo first
quickstartSteps.note: The default path starts with the strongest supported production story.

quickstartSteps.title: Generate managed target files
quickstartSteps.note: Generate the managed target artifacts from one authored repo.

quickstartSteps.title: Validate before handoff
quickstartSteps.note: Use the strict validation gate before CI, teammate handoff, or release.

supportLanes.name: Claude plugin package
supportLanes.note: Production-ready for the stable default hook subset. Settings and manifest support are solid; some wider Claude package surfaces still stay beta.

supportLanes.name: Codex runtime and package lanes
supportLanes.note: The Codex notify runtime lane and the official Codex package lane are both production-ready today.

supportLanes.name: Gemini extension lane
supportLanes.note: Gemini extension packaging is production-ready, and the Go runtime lane is production-ready for the current stable 9-hook contract.

supportLanes.name: OpenCode workspace-config lane
supportLanes.note: Repo-local config, MCP, skills, commands, agents, themes, and plugin refs are supported, but this is still a workspace-config target, not a production-ready runtime lane.

supportLanes.name: Cursor workspace-config lane
supportLanes.note: Project rules and MCP config are covered, while root CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md stay reserved for plugin boundary docs; Cursor remains a strict workspace-config lane.
